So next thing I want to talk about chain of thought prompting. So chain of thought prompting
is a very popular way of prompting models. And the way it works is you get the model to
explain its reasoning before making an answer. And so sorry, this example is a little bit
small, but like the standard prompting method is like Roger has five tennis balls. He buys
two more cans of tennis balls. Each can has three tennis balls. How many tennis balls
does he have now? The answer is 11. And so this is an in context example. And then you
have your input, which has a different problem. The cafeteria has 23 apples. If they use
20 to make lunch and bought six more, how many apples do they have? The answer is 27.
And so this is wrong. So what chain of thought prompting does is instead of just giving
the answer, it gives you an additional reasoning chain that says Roger started with five balls,
two cans of three tennis balls. Each is six tennis balls, five plus six equals 11. The
answer is 11. And so then when you feed this in, basically the model will generate a similar
reasoning chain. And then it's more likely to get the answer correct. And this very robustly
works for many different problems where a reasoning chain is necessary. And if you think
about the reason why this, why this works, I think there's basically two reasons why.
The first reason is I only wrote one on the finger, but the first reason is it allows the
model to decompose harder problems into simpler problems and simpler problems are easier
to use it. So instead of immediately trying to solve the whole problem in a single go,
it will first solve the problem of like how many are left after you use money. And so it
gets three. And so now it has this three here. So now it can solve the next problem adding
sex that's equal to 90. So it's solving simpler, subproblems than it is in compared to
everyone's. Another reason why is it allows for adaptive computation time. So if you think
about like a transformer model, if you think about a transformer model, a transformer model
has fixed computation time. You were predicting you showed it right. You have a fixed number of
layers. And based on that fixed number of layers, it passes all the information through
the matrix of prediction. And some problems are harder than others, right? So it would be
very wasteful to have a really big transformer that could solve, you know, really complex math
problems. In the same amount of time, it takes to predict that the next word is like a dog
after the word the big or something like that, right? So there are some things that are easy.
We can do it a second. There are some things that take us more time. And essentially this
chain of thought reasoning is doing that. It's giving it more time to solve the harder problems.
